common.siteDescription: A living memorial for the victims of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1979–present)

home.heroSubtitle: A living memorial for the victims of the Islamic Republic of Iran.
home.heroDescription: Since 1979, the Islamic Republic has systematically silenced, imprisoned, tortured, and killed its own people. This memorial exists so that no victim is forgotten.
home.searchPlaceholder: Search by name, place, or event...
home.submitInfoDescription: Help us document the victims. Every name, every story, every detail matters.
home.whyMattersText: Behind every number is a person. A father, a student, a child. This memorial restores their identity — so the world remembers them not as statistics, but as human beings.

submit.title: Submit Information
submit.description: Help us document the victims. Every name matters.
submit.mediaHint: JPG, PNG, WebP, GIF, MP4, MOV, or WebM — max 20 MB per file, up to 5 files. Only upload media you have the right to share.
submit.detailsHint: What do you know about this person? Date of birth, place of death, circumstances, family — everything helps.
submit.sourcesHint: Links to news articles, social media posts, or human rights organization reports.
submit.privacyNote: Your information is kept confidential and will not be published.
submit.thankYou: Thank you. Your submission will be reviewed.
submit.error: Something went wrong. Please try again.
submit.aliasesHint: Comma-separated. Names this person was also known by (e.g. Jina, Zhina).
submit.quotesHint: One quote per line. Things this person said or wrote.
submit.onlineHint: Only include accounts that are confirmed to belong to this person.

about.title: About This Memorial
about.quote: "As long as we remember, they live on."
about.missionText: This memorial exists to preserve the memory of every person killed, executed, imprisoned, or disappeared by the Islamic Republic of Iran since 1979. Every victim was a human being — with a name, a story, dreams, and people who loved them. They deserve to be remembered as people, not as statistics.
about.familyWishLead: Many of the families we hear from tell us the same thing:
about.familyWishQuote: We do not want the resistance against the regime that killed our loved ones to die alongside them. Otherwise they will have died for nothing.
about.familyWishBody: Our second commitment flows directly from this wish. We build the archive to be transparent, citable, machine-readable, and freely reusable — so that researchers, journalists, lawyers, and human-rights organizations can carry forward, in the families' name, the truth-telling work the families themselves have asked for. Transparency here is a service rendered to the people who lost someone — not a political agenda of ours.
about.whyText: Since 1979, the Islamic Republic has persecuted and killed dissidents, ethnic and religious minorities, women, students, and ordinary citizens. From the mass executions of the 1980s to the chain murders of the 1990s, from the 2019 crackdown to the Woman, Life, Freedom protests of 2022 — victims are too often forgotten, their stories left untold. The official record is silent or hostile; private grief is left without a public home. This memorial is that home.
about.audiencesLead: Iran Memorial serves several audiences, and we design every feature with them in mind:
about.audienceFamilies: Families and loved ones — a respectful, lasting place to mourn, to share, and to add what only they know about the person they lost.
about.audienceResearchers: Researchers and historians — primary-source data with full provenance, exportable in standard formats for academic use.
about.audienceJournalists: Journalists — citable profiles, verified sources, machine-readable bulk export, embeddable widgets.
about.audienceLawyers: Human-rights lawyers and accountability bodies — structured case data compatible with HURIDOCS and ICC-style evidentiary workflows.
about.audienceNgos: NGOs and partner organizations — open API and CC BY-SA 4.0 licensing for free reuse in their own platforms.
about.audienceDiaspora: The Iranian diaspora — a multilingual, RTL-correct archive that connects communities across 16 languages.
about.sourcesLead: The archive is compiled from 15+ independent human-rights data sources, each cross-referenced and annotated with a credibility tier. No record relies on a single source.
about.source_ohchr: UN Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights
about.methodologyLead: Every record passes through cross-referencing before it appears on a profile page. We mark each record with one of three verification tiers, and we never hide which tier a record sits in:
about.methodologyTierVerified: Verified — corroborated by at least two independent sources of high or reputable credibility, including a primary human-rights organization report or a court document.
about.methodologyTierUnverified: Unverified — present in a community submission or a single source, awaiting cross-reference.
about.methodologyTierDisputed: Disputed — sources disagree on key facts (date, cause, identity). The disagreement is shown openly on the profile.
about.methodologyTransparency: Source-level transparency: every fact on every profile carries a citation back to the originating source. Our deduplication, photo-mirroring, and source-credibility pipelines are open-source — anyone can audit how the data is processed.
about.standardsLead: So that the records hold up under scrutiny — in any forum, on any platform, for as long as the web exists — we hold ourselves to the highest open-data standards we can find. This is a commitment to the families: the record of their loved one will remain useful long after this site, and long after us.
about.standardsSchemaOrgDesc: Every victim profile emits a structured Person record so search engines and AI agents read it natively.
about.standardsDatasetDesc: The archive is published as a Dataset with versions, license, and machine-readable distributions — discoverable in Google Dataset Search.
about.standardsFairDesc: Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable. Persistent identifiers, CC BY-SA 4.0 licensing, full provenance per record.
about.standardsHuridocsDesc: The interchange standard used by Amnesty International, FIDH, and OHCHR-style organizations. Export endpoint on the roadmap.
about.standardsApiDesc: Public, rate-limited /api/v1 plus a bulk JSON dump plus an MCP server, so any developer, journalist, or AI agent can query the archive directly.
about.standardsLicenseDesc: All data is free to reuse with attribution and share-alike. Free does not mean unaccountable — every claim is sourced and citable.
about.apiLead: We publish the archive in three reusable forms so that partner organizations and independent researchers never have to scrape the site:
about.apiRest: REST API — /api/v1, JSON responses, 1,000 requests/hour, full OpenAPI spec.
about.apiMcp: MCP server — five tools for AI agents (search, profile, executions, death-row, statistics), no authentication needed.
about.apiBulk: Bulk dataset — a single ~30 MB JSON of every record, licensed CC BY-SA 4.0, for offline use and archival mirrors.
about.howToHelpFamilies: If you are a family member or witness: submit what you know about your loved one — names, photos, stories, court documents, the things only you remember. Every detail matters.
about.howToHelpCorrect: Correct or expand existing profiles where information is missing, outdated, or wrong.
about.howToHelpTranslate: Help translate content into the 16 supported languages — particularly Arabic, Italian, Spanish, Hebrew, Russian, and Turkish, which currently lag behind.
about.howToHelpResearchers: If you are a researcher or journalist: use the archive, cite it, and let us know what data you wish were structured. We add fields based on real research needs.
about.howToHelpNgos: If you work for a human-rights organization: contact us about HURIDOCS-style integration or data partnership. Our license already permits free reuse.
about.howToHelpDev: If you are a developer: the project is open source. Issues, PRs, and reviews are welcome.
about.howToHelpShare: Share the project — every additional reader is one more person carrying the names forward.
about.privacyText: We treat the data of victims and their families with care. Submitted information is reviewed before publication. Contributors' identities are never disclosed. Sensitive details (locations of living relatives, ongoing legal cases) are redacted at the request of family members. We document facts and source them transparently; we do not editorialize on individual cases. Families can request changes or removal of a profile at any time.
about.openSourceText: Code is open source under the MIT license. Data is published under CC BY-SA 4.0. Every commit, every issue, every change to the archive is auditable in the public repository. The project is non-commercial, has no investors, and runs no advertising.

developers.title: API Documentation
developers.description: Access Iran Memorial data programmatically with our REST API. Designed for journalists, researchers, and human rights organizations.
developers.step1: Request an API key from the admin team ({contactEmail})
developers.step2: Include your key in the Authorization header: Bearer iran_mem_...
developers.license: Data License: CC BY-SA 4.0 - Attribution required
developers.mcpIntro: Connect any MCP-aware LLM (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline) directly to the Memorial database. No API key, no auth — strictly read-only at the Postgres layer.

press.title: Press Kit
press.intro: Citable statistics, embeddable counter, photography credits, and a direct line to the project. Everything you need to cover Iran Memorial accurately.
press.boilerplate1: is an independent, community-driven, non-commercial memorial documenting victims of the Islamic Republic of Iran since 1979.
press.boilerplate2: The platform aggregates 12 verified data sources, applies cross-referencing and deduplication, and publishes the result under CC BY-SA 4.0 for journalism, research, and accountability work. Maintained as a programme of Woman Life Freedom e.V.
press.oneLiner: Iran Memorial — independent open archive documenting {count}+ victims of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1979–present).
press.statsIntro: All numbers update from the live database. Free to embed, cite, or query via API.
press.widgetIntro: Drop this iframe into any article, NGO site, or activist page. Updates automatically. No tracking, no JS.
press.latestIntro: Five most recently verified profiles — useful for "who was just documented" stories.
press.brandIntro: When citing or reproducing data, please use the project name in full and link to the source. Two licences apply (code MIT, data CC BY-SA 4.0).
press.citationIntro: For papers, court briefs, and policy submissions:
press.citationDoi: Permanent DOIs published monthly via Zenodo. Methodology and provenance:
press.contactIntro: Same address handles press requests, partnership questions, and security disclosures. We answer humans, not automated systems.
press.contactSignalNote: Available on request for sensitive sources
press.contactResponseNote: Within 48 hours for verified press; same-day for time-sensitive stories with deadline
press.contactConfidential: We treat sources confidentially. Iran-based contacts are routed through encrypted channels by default.
press.devPartnerIntro: Three deeper paths if you're building on the dataset:
press.devCardApiDesc: Public read-only endpoints, MCP server, OpenAPI spec.
press.devCardMethodDesc: Verification tiers, dedup logic, source registry.
press.devCardSourcesDesc: All 12 contributing organizations with credibility tiers.

embedPreview.title: Embed the live counter
embedPreview.intro: Drop the iframe snippet on your site to surface live victim counts from the Iran Memorial database. Updates automatically as new records are documented and verified.
embedPreview.step2: Click Show snippet and copy the iframe.
embedPreview.step3: Paste anywhere HTML is allowed (CMS, Notion, Webflow, plain HTML).
embedPreview.credit: Please link back to the source.

credibility.reputable_tooltip: Established media outlet or vetted memorial project
credibility.community_tooltip: Open submission, social media, or community archive — corroborate with another source

methodology.title: Verification Methodology
methodology.intro: Iran Memorial collects victim records from twelve independent sources and ranks each one by credibility. This page explains exactly how we verify entries, deduplicate, and update the database — so journalists, researchers, and families can trust what they see.
methodology.tiersIntro: Every source in our registry maps to one of three public tiers. The colored badge next to each citation on a victim profile reflects this tier directly.
methodology.tierHighBody: Established human-rights organizations and UN bodies that publish primary investigations, court documents, and victim biographies under their own name and editorial standards.
methodology.tierReputableBody: Established media outlets and vetted memorial projects that aggregate, translate, or report on primary sources with editorial review.
methodology.tierCommunityBody: Open submissions, social-media archives, and community-maintained lists. Useful as a tip for further investigation; we treat them as unverified until corroborated.
methodology.autoVerifyIntro: A victim record is marked verified automatically when at least one of the following is true:
methodology.autoVerifyRuleA: One or more High-credibility sources cite the victim by name (e.g. Boroumand, Amnesty, IHR, HRANA, UN FFM).
methodology.autoVerifyRuleB: Two or more Reputable-tier sources cite the victim independently.
methodology.autoVerifyRuleC: Three or more sources of any tier cite the victim, after deduplication.
methodology.autoVerifyNote: Records that fail all three rules remain marked unverified — visible on the site, but flagged so visitors and researchers can apply their own judgment.
methodology.registryIntro: The complete list of organizations whose records we ingest. New sources are added with a public commit and a row in this table.
methodology.dedupIntro: Many sources document the same victim with slight name variations or differing date precision. We run a deterministic dedup pass that scores candidate pairs and merges high-confidence matches.
methodology.dedupBullet1: Name normalization (transliteration variants, with/without patronymic, RTL/LTR).
methodology.dedupBullet2: Date-of-death match within a configurable window (exact day, then ±7 days for partial dates).
methodology.dedupBullet3: Score bands: ≥70 auto-merge, 50–69 manual review, <50 keep separate.
methodology.dedupBullet4: Every merge preserves the original sources of both records — citation history is never lost.
methodology.photoIntro: A memorial only works if the photos it shows remain available. We don't hot-link to external CDNs; every victim photo is mirrored to memorial-controlled storage with the source URL preserved for provenance.
methodology.photoBullet1: Mirror: every external photo is copied to our own filesystem on first sight. The served URL becomes /photos/<id>; the original URL is archived in a separate column.
methodology.photoBullet2: Provenance preserved: original source URLs (Telegram CDN, Supabase, news sites) are kept alongside the local copy so attribution and re-mirror remain possible.
methodology.photoBullet3: Perceptual deduplication: every mirrored file gets a 64-bit perceptual hash (pHash) and a SHA-256. Exact duplicates are hardlinked on disk; visually similar photos (Hamming distance ≤ 8) are flagged for admin review to prevent stock-photo reuse and to surface other photos of the same person.
methodology.photoBullet4: Health monitoring: a weekly job HEAD-checks every external URL and marks broken ones (e.g. expired Telegram CDN links) so the frontend filters them out instead of rendering broken-image icons.
methodology.photoBullet5: Graceful fallback: when a photo cannot be loaded at all, a candle placeholder appears in its place. No broken-image icons, ever.
methodology.workflowIntro: Three independent crawlers run nightly on our infrastructure. Each one is idempotent, resumes from checkpoint on failure, and only inserts records that pass the source-specific schema.
methodology.cronWitness: Crawl new posts on witness.report and ingest victim photos + biographical metadata.
methodology.cronHrana: Pull new HRANA reports (executions, deaths in custody, killed protesters).
methodology.cronHengaw: Crawl Hengaw bulletins (Kurdish-region incidents, prisoner executions).
methodology.workflowNote: Human review of merge candidates and new sources happens weekly. The full pipeline is open source.
methodology.limitsIntro: We document what we know honestly, including the gaps:
methodology.limitsBullet1: Pre-2000 records depend heavily on the Boroumand archive — under-counting in regions where that archive is thin is likely.
methodology.limitsBullet2: Names are de-transliterated in multiple ways across sources; some duplicates still slip past dedup until manually flagged.
methodology.limitsBullet3: Some 1988 mass-execution victims are documented only as a count without identifiable names; they are tracked separately under Anonymous Victims.

roadmap.title: Roadmap
roadmap.intro: Public list of what Iran Memorial is working on right now, what is committed for the next 1–2 quarters, and what is on the radar but not yet timed. We publish this so partners, donors, and the community can plan around it — and so we hold ourselves to it.
roadmap.nowIntro: In active development this quarter. If you can help on any of these, talk to us — most have specific dependencies tagged.
roadmap.nextIntro: Committed for the next 1–2 quarters. The order can shift based on partner availability and funding.
roadmap.laterIntro: On the radar, no committed timeline. Often blocked on partners, funding, or a specific volunteer skill.
roadmap.doneIntro: Kept here for one quarter so partners can verify what changed and when. Older items roll off into CHANGELOG.md.
roadmap.supportTitle: How you can move items forward
roadmap.supportIntro: Roadmap items move faster when one of these arrives. Each item is tagged with what it specifically needs.
roadmap.supportFundingDesc: Donations to Woman Life Freedom e.V. earmarked for Iran Memorial unblock infrastructure costs and translator stipends.
roadmap.supportPartnersDesc: NGO co-signatures on sanctions submissions, academic affiliations for the methodology pre-print, legal partners for jurisdictional resilience.
roadmap.supportVolunteersDesc: Translators, researchers, developers, family liaisons. Start by
